speaker: interviewer
text: --1996. Did you have any idea of how the war was progressing while you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know that we didn't know the dates. We didn't know what time of the day it is. It was not that important either. Maybe people who were older, they manage-- often I hear now that the people knew when Jewish holidays was. They said the prayers. Well, I lived more from a moment to the moment, always with the attitude, not even fear, attitude that I'm going to be shot or sent to the oven because-- nor did I have a sufficient fat on me before the war, never mind during the camp. And that was really a first-class [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] requirement, that if you didn't have any fat-- now I'm-- miss, I'm terribly emotional now. If you did not look sort of more or less human, well, then you were shot or you were sent to the oven. And that's how constantly we were picked from the group who did not look the so-called best of the lot. Well, they were going to the oven. We knew that. If somebody was sick, if somebody couldn't walk properly, they were pulled out.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have never seen them before and never after-- never after the war. So where are they? They went to the oven all right. And there were ovens, believe me. We smelled burning bodies nonstop. And we looked at the tall chimneys, and there was smoke coming out. So-- What happened from Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Auschwitz, one night, 17 of January, somehow [? it went-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17 of January, 1945. We were told it's 17 of January. We were put into the cattle trucks again-- pushed. Quick, quick, quick, the Russians are coming. I must tell you, one evening just before we were taken out of Auschwitz, it was-- we were sent quickly to the blocks where we slept, where we lived, and we have seen the sky lit up beautifully like daylight. And we were told that these are Stalin candles, they were called. They have lit up the whole camp, and they were bombing the SS blocks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took us out of that that we shouldn't be bombed out of that enclosure where the working shops, workshops, were. And we were told that Stalin candles-- you know, who got the information from whom I shall never know, and it was unimportant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Important was that it's Stalin candles, and the Russians are very close by. And that gave us so much strength. Well, if the Russians are so close, they're standing on a [NON-ENGLISH]-- on a [NON-ENGLISH] edge. Well, they will save us, and we're still alive. Auschwitz, no Auschwitz, we're alive. But that wasn't to be. They have put us into the cattle trucks and send us off. On and on and on we went. You know, if it was a week or two weeks, to sit in the cattle trucks. And some of the trucks were bombed, so we had to get out of one truck into another truck. Eventually we arrived and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions in the cattle truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Have you ever seen cattle driving into the slaughterhouse? That were our condition. Occasionally they would open the sliding door and thrown in bucket of some sort of a grain, some rice or-- or buckwheat or whatever it was. And we would all run for it and try to eat it. And some people were dead. They couldn't eat it anymore. They didn't give a damn. Because we were packed into those places so tight that there was no chance to breathe. Again, I worked my way to that window and watched where we're going. Not that I knew where I was, because we were already by then close to-- in-- in Germany we were and going towards Bergen-Belsen. Well, I didn't know where Bergen-Belsen was exactly in Germany. But the fact that you were near the window, you had a little bit more air. The rest of the truck was freezing cold. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We arrive in Bergen-Belsen. We were not told where we're going, why we're going there. We walked and walked and walked. And as we were walking, on one side, on the left-hand side of the driveway, there were people standing like in big cages with a barbed wire enclosed, or some wire enclosed, that we could not go there, and they couldn't come out. Later I found out that they were people from Budapest, from Hungary, with Swiss papers. How they obtain, I don't know, but probably for money. And they were told that they should stand there because they will be transported for-- to Switzerland. After the war, I have found out that that-- those people were the [NON-ENGLISH] to be exchanged for [NON-ENGLISH], for dollars, for whatever. But what eventually has happened to them, I don't know. The only thing it was of interest to me to look if my sister is there, because I knew that my sister was somewhere-- or in Czechoslovakia or in Budapest. So I thought, well, she may be amongst them. So I peered in. I spoke to the girls who were in my transport. They don't even remember. To that extent, we were bamboozled that we didn't what was going on. They didn't remember. To me, it was important to look inside those cages for my sister. We arrive in Bergen-Belsen, in the camp itself. It was a horrifying sight. Corpses walking, walking corpses. Everyone with diarrhea, with black running on their legs. No-- the lower part of the clothing stripped completely, because they were running from the block where they slept to the trenches where they were-- there were no latrines. That's where-- where doing their business. Some of them didn't even go back to the block. They were sitting there where they were, and that's where they died. And as we went, we saw all that. Some people were just dragged out of the block where we slept. And if the piece of bread fell out of that blanket they were covered with, girls around to grab that piece of bread with the typhus in it. Well, it was no place to stay. Really it was no place to stay. And one of the SS women, whoever it was, it could have been Hilde-- [? Ilse ?] [? Kramer, ?] because she was in that particular camp. That's the one you probably heard that she used the lampshades from human skin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They were ugly-looking SS women, ugly. Some of them were good-looking and pleasant, and I thought to myself, why would she want to be SS? She's German. She could be anything. Why does she want to be in the camp? It was never answered in my mind. It was-- I never could find reason for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in that particular camp, in Bergen-Belsen, somebody came and called for people to work who had just arrive. A few days, I said, all right, I'm going to work. First of all, I get some clothes, and we get something to eat. So we volunteered. A few girls are still alive here today in Sydney. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] was in that group.

speaker: interviewee
text: We volunteer to go. Where? We didn't know, but it didn't matter. We ended up, after long traveling, in place called Venusberg. It was close to a plane factory. They were making parts of plane. How did you get to Venusberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cattle trucks. That-- that was the only mode of transport. Sometimes we had to get out, walk over the bombed tracks to another cattle truck, which was prepared for. And sometimes we travel all around the way to Bavaria and god knows where to get to that particular spot. That Venusberg place was quite nice, relatively speaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of place? What kind of a camp was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very small place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had-- in the woods there was the plane factory, and that's where the girls went to work. I already had typhus. I developed typhus, and I went to-- to-- to stay on the bunk, and I stayed on the bunk for days. And the girls who knew me and whom-- with whom we were close kept coming in the evening-- if I want something to eat, if I want my soup. If I didn't want my soup, they took it. So they had a little bit more to eat. That was the attitude, not to waste anything. And I remember at one stage somebody covered me with a paper. And who was covered with a paper was taken out in the morning on the pile, wherever they were putting the body. But I got rid of that paper, and I was still alive. I realized-- I must have realized I was hallucinating. I was talking all to myself or dreaming. That's when I remember I saw my mother coming up, getting up from the block. And later on I found out that what I was seeing then, being so very ill, it was the truth. Mother was in one of the [? bombed ?] huts in-- in her camp, but she survived. She went out of it and survive. After the war, we got together and we talked about it. And mother said, yes, that happened to me. You know, it's sort of things like that gave you strength to go on, after I have seen my mother alive, thinking that she was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to Venusberg, how long were you ill?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly, but not very long. All I know that by the time I was in Venusberg, I had again stockings and shoes and dress and coat. And we were again put into the cattle trucks to go to Mauthausen. But of course, we didn't know we were going to Mauthausen. The reason was that some of the army pushing-- the American Army or the English Army or the Russian Army were-- this-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were approaching, and they had to disband. The Germans had to disband the factory. So they put us in the cattle trucks, and again we were traveling-- walking and walking and traveling and walking--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the journey.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's 50 years. It's very not terribly sharp in my mind. All I know that we were walking on and on and on. And I was always trying to be in the front, because in the front you had chance of surviving. When you went at the end of that long march, you were losing strength. You didn't have that initiative to walk on, and you were shot and into the ditch. And sometimes girls tried to run away from that-- from that march, because we were told schnell, schnell, quick, quick, the Russians approaching or the Americans approaching. So of course, that gave us the idea to run away, to get into that forest to hide. Some did. I have heard afterwards that some people managed to escape, and they were liberated earlier. I wasn't. We went on and on for days and days and no food and no water and nothing-- no toilet. One night I remember we went into a barn to sleep. After many days, the Germans who were surrounding us decided that we're going into that village to sleep. Probably they didn't know anymore where to take us, how to get on with it. We went to sleep and so did the Germans. And they took their shoes off. And that's all I needed to see-- a good leather pair of shoes. By that time, the wooden shoes that I had have fallen apart. I have put rags on my feet. It was terrible-- blistered, bloody feet. And when I saw the pair of shoes, I thought opportunity to get these shoes for myself. I could only fit into his shoes. I took the pair of shoes. I went to-- to sleep to the other end of the block. I wrapped it up with the rags I had over the wooden [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] shoes. And I hear in the morning that soldier looking for his shoes left, right, and center. Eventually he went to [? lorry ?] and got himself another pair of shoes. And he sort of didn't search that much. And the war was so much at the end. It must have been April, that it didn't matter anymore. They already were so very destroyed. They realized that this is the end. We were ushered again to some trucks. And we ended up going on foot out of the trucks up the hill to Mauthausen. We arrive in Mauthausen into quadrangle. It was up the hill facing four ovens. And we knew they were ovens, and they told us they were ovens. But they also explained that unfortunately we have to wait because a courier went to somewhere into Germany to get Zyklon B. They run out of Zyklon B.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what that was for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to-- to destroy our bodies after we were shot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Zyklon B-- we knew about Zyklon B, that they were necessary for the burning of the bodies. What that Zyklon B did to the bodies, I don't know to this day, but that much we did know. They were probably shooting us first, because they were walking-- it was like a huge wall, fairly wide-- because they were walking all around us. And that's where the news came from that, sorry, we have to wait. They can't start shooting till-- till the Zyklon B will arrive. But in the meantime, they were throwing to us, like to animals, chocolate, figs, cocoa in tins, sardines in a small quantity. But we stood in front of the oven, and yet we bend to pick up whatever the food was.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, that I cannot reconcile with how-- what did they do to our psyche, to our thinking, to our behavior that we stood in front of the oven and we're bent to pick up the piece of chocolate which was lying on the floor? We were still capable of picking it up. I guess we were hungry to that extent that it didn't matter anymore. We were completely destroyed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [SIGHS] next morning they said, you all have to go into [NON-ENGLISH], into the gypsy compound. They took us to the gypsy compound. That was already the May-- May in Mauthausen. They locked us in, and as far as I remember, that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lying on the floor [INAUDIBLE] straw and next to me Hellga Seigal lying and looking at me and sort of seeing me dying. How long? I don't remember, but it couldn't have been very long. At certain point she said to me, don't die. The Americans are here. I thought, well, her or me, one of us gone mad, because how could they come into the gypsy compound, Americans? And I lost completely confidence in all the Russian Army, English Army, American Army. They didn't come to save us. They could have done early. They didn't. So who are we that they should worry about us? That was firmly implanted in my mind. It was just us, ourselves. We could save ourself, not rely on anybody else. They have destroyed us mentally, emotionally, de-humanize. You know, to-- to bend to pick up some food standing in front of the oven, and the doors were wide enough for you to walk in. I'm not even sure that they would have shoot us before we walked in there. How they were going to do it? It was a huge compound. I believe it's still standing. So when Hellga bent to me, put her cheek to my mouth to feel if I'm still breathing, I couldn't talk, but I clamped her cheek with my teeth to let her know that I'm still alive. And after certain time elapsed, how long I don't know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I saw, you know, figures walking into that camp and-- and beautiful white clouds, and they were all white.

speaker: interviewee
text: All I saw the cloud and the white figures through that dying process. I thought, well, that's it. I have died, and these are angels. I'm in heaven. So because I went through the camp, I deserve to go to heaven, and I went to heaven. The fact that I was not yet school, and I did lots of other things before the war, I excused myself. I was in heaven. Only to find out that that were American soldiers spreading DDT before they have allowed us to get out of that compound, because everybody had full of lice and full of typhus. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my angels proved to be an American soldiers who came to liberate us in Mauthausen. Still don't remember much more from that moment till I was in the upper part of Mauthausen in the hospital part. [SIGHS] We're lying in a bed. Some of us had drips. Some of us were fed with vitamins. But we didn't get anything to eat by mouth. We were really treated well. And I remembered one stage, some soldier took me out in his arms outside to be photographed. And I was very conscious of the fact that what I was wearing-- the nightdress was open at the back, and they will see my legs and my bottom. It's crazy, isn't it, that sort of one still functions to some extent. And later on, I met somebody, told me that Hellga is in another room, also in a bed, dying. I went to Hellga. I spoke to her. I pulled her out of that dying process. She survived the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in July, we were put into army trucks, American army trucks, and taken to Czechoslovakia. In Czechoslovakia, we stopped to be given soup. But all the time I had the shoes on my feet. I was saving those shoes. How it preserved from the gypsy compound? I could never get shoes to fit my feet. So that was a enormous find.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we were on this truck, a friend of mine had terribly swollen feet. And I took the shoes off and gave it to her so she could arrive in Poland in proper shoes. I would say that I had another pair of shoes given by Joint people that came to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] re-clothe us, to give us clothes, decent clothes, to go home in. And they gave me a navy pair of shoes, part material, part leather with a little heel. And I preserved those shoes. I thought [? Ilka ?] should have those shoes. She's here in Sydney. Constantly I tease her about those shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Czechoslovakia, where we were given beautiful soup and bread, to Poland, to Cracow. In Cracow I have found out that my sister is alive. So I stayed three or four days in Cracow. And I put my few bits and pieces, and I went to Budapest by train. I arrive in Budapest without language, without knowing anybody, to look for my sister. You know, it's like you would go to Beijing without Chinese language to see where the-- where your sister lives. I sure enough, somehow I have found my brother-in-law's brother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He looked at me, he took me to his place, open his wardrobe, tried to give me some of his wife's clothes, who was a lovely, petite female, well-dressed. And there were shoes that [NON-ENGLISH] with wedges, wedge shoes. [SIGHS] I couldn't fit into any of them. I said, [NON-ENGLISH], please take me the way I am. He was going to take me for lunch. Take me. I'll walk behind you so you don't have to be embarrassed, but I want to eat. [SIGHS] He took me to a very nice restaurant. It was practically empty, but on each table was a basket with bread rolls. So you know, I had such a mixed feelings because those bread rolls-- that boy who got shot came immediately to my mind. And that's how it is to my whole life. The moment I see something, it brings camp to me. And I have to [? dissuade ?] myself from thinking about it and get attention to something else. It-- it just horrifying, but that's-- all my life is like that. I must not start dwelling upon anything that resembles or bring camp to my mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have eaten-- I don't remember till this day what I have-- what I was given to eat. I even taste of it. It's in my mouth. And there were those bread rolls. And I said, [NON-ENGLISH], could I have those other bread rolls as well? And he said, no, you won't be hungry ever again. Don't have-- don't take any bread rolls. Probably he was embarrassed, because he didn't know what hunger means.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could not reconcile with it. I wanted to go to that restaurant again to get those bread rolls. You know, that time, I could not restrain myself. I have seen piece of soap. I felt I better take it. It was called organization, and one had to organize things. That's how we live. That's how--

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when did you find out that your mother was alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Poland, to Cracow, to look who else survive, who came back. At that stage, we had hopes that my brother will come back from Russia. I did meet somebody who came back from Russia and told me, don't wait for your brother because he's not alive. I know somebody who live in [NON-ENGLISH] who knew him personally before the war, during the war. So that was pretty reliable person. And then somebody else came and told me, your mother is on the way back. She survived the war in [NON-ENGLISH], and we were together in [NON-ENGLISH]. She is coming back. So I stayed few days, and mother arrive. I took mother with me to Budapest to be with my sister. How could I explain to you that once emotions-- it-- it was dead. It was not-- we were [? nobody ?] rejoicing. There was not a party. My mother came back from the dead. Only I knew the-- the-- the circumstances she went away in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first thing, she ask about father. And that, again, I couldn't come out with it. How could I tell her that my father was given [? naphtha ?] injection shortly after she left? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was terrible. It was terrible. We're going to stop here to change the tape.
